explanation_md: With a virtual destructor in `Base`, `delete p` dispatches to `Derived::~Derived` first (most-derived), then automatically chains to `Base::~Base`. Destructors always run in reverse construction order.

prompt_md: What happens when you `delete` a `Base*` pointer that actually points to a `Derived` object, if `Base` has no virtual destructor?

options.a: Both `Base` and `Derived` destructors run in correct order.
options.c: Only `Base::~Base` runs; `Derived::~Derived` is never called, leading to resource leaks or undefined behavior.

explanation_md: Without a virtual destructor, `delete` uses the static type of the pointer (`Base*`) to decide which destructor to call. Only `Base::~Base` runs. `Derived::~Derived` is skipped, leaking any resources owned by `Derived`. This is undefined behavior when the dynamic type differs from the static type.

When `d` goes out of scope, C++ always calls destructors from most-derived to base — regardless of whether destructors are virtual. The virtual destructor is only needed when deleting through a *base pointer*. When the static type is `Derived`, both destructors run in correct order automatically.

prompt_md: Which idiom correctly declares a virtual destructor with no custom body in a base class?
explanation_md: `virtual ~Base() = default;` asks the compiler to generate the default (empty) destructor body while marking it virtual. Option (a) would produce a link error — the definition is required. Option (b) would make the class abstract (pure virtual destructor). Option (d) uses `override` which applies to derived classes overriding a base, not to defining the base destructor itself.
